full_response:
We wish to find, for each 2-dimensional space, the area enclosed by a geodesic “circle,” i.e. the locus of points whose geodesic distance from a chosen origin is R.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Euclidean plane  

Metric (in polar coordinates):
ds² = dr² + r² dφ² .

The determinant of the metric tensor is
g = det diag(1, r²) = r² ,
so the area element is  

dA = √g dr dφ = r dr dφ .

Area inside r = R:

A_E(R) = ∫₀^{2π} dφ ∫₀^{R} r dr
        = 2π [½ r²]₀^{R}
        = π R² .
--------------------------------------------------
2.  Lobachevsky (hyperbolic) plane  

Metric (in “polar–type’’ coordinates):
ds² = du² + cosh²(u) dv² .

Here u is the proper radial distance, because the radial part of the line element is just du².

Determinant of the metric:
g = det diag(1, cosh²u) = cosh²u ,
so the area element is  

dA = √g du dv = cosh u du dv .

Area inside u = R:

A_H(R) = ∫_{v=0}^{2π} dv ∫_{u=0}^{R} cosh u du
        = 2π [sinh u]₀^{R}
        = 2π sinh R .
--------------------------------------------------

Thus the areas grow differently with radius:

\boxed{A_{\text{Euclidean}}(R) = \pi R^{2}, \qquad
       A_{\text{Lobachevsky}}(R) = 2\pi \sinh R}

(The same symbol R denotes the proper geodesic radius in each space.)
